metadata.title: Claude, In Your Voice — bot vs bot, season one · 4/7
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity UI beats; exhibits keep their own look (MUSINIQUE.md graded-exhibit law).
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Greek (one-word budget — Musinique persona). Wagwan stays Bear's.
metadata.charter: MUSINIQUE.md — fights for the indie musician; receipts law; roll-your-own law; no fake numbers
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW: UI at B00 (with RESULT lines) / verdict / handoff / outro only; each inner beat carries an illustration spec in props.illustration (kind: concept | pattern | code | exhibit) — build per medhavy_com/youtube/mcp/remotion-src worked example + skills/animated-deck patterns. ROLL-YOUR-OWN LAW: the handoff paste is a deck piece the viewer keeps. RECEIPTS LAW: claims trace to RECEIPTS.md beside the playlist; illustrative examples labeled as such.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: I get that the brief, not the model, separates wallpaper from a caption I'd claim, and that lyrics need their own file because craft rules aren't caption rules. Help me write both: (1) build my caption voice file with the banned list, platform defaults, and the two tripwires that flag numbers and ask before naming a person; (2) build a separate lyric file encoding syllable cadence, rhyme scheme, and open-vowel chorus endings; (3) tell me exactly what to check in my distributor's current AI-disclosure and copyright language before anything touches a master.

props.command: I get that the brief, not the model, separates wallpaper from a caption I'd claim, and that lyrics need their own file because craft rules aren't caption rules. Help me write both: (1) build my caption voice file with the banned list, platform defaults, and the two tripwires that flag numbers and ask before naming a person; (2) build a separate lyric file encoding syllable cadence, rhyme scheme, and open-vowel chorus endings; (3) tell me exactly what to check in my distributor's current AI-disclosure and copyright language before anything touches a master.
output: a voice file: tone, banned words, platform defaults
specificity is the lever — context in, craft out
numeric claims and named people: flagged, never auto-posted

beats.narration_text: Here's the file, and notice what it actually is: your taste, written down. The tone you keep. The words you'd never say — every artist has a banned list; write yours. Platform defaults, so you stop re-explaining Instagram to a machine every Tuesday. And two tripwires baked in: it asks before naming a person, and it flags — never posts — anything with a number in it.

illustration.spec: Onda code-block: the artist-brand-voice skill file — tone; never-use list ('eclectic', 'journey'); platform defaults (IG 2-3 sentences, X under 200 chars no hashtags, LinkedIn third-person for sync); ask-before-naming-people; flag-don't-post numeric claims

beats.narration_text: Then the working truth nobody sells you: the difference between a forgettable draft and a usable one is almost never the model. It's the brief. 'Write a caption' gets you wallpaper. Your genre, your tempo, the constraint, the banned list — that gets you a sentence you'd actually claim. Same machine. Different you in it.

illustration.spec: same model, split at 'what you told it': vague brief -> generic caption (demos fine, sounds like everyone); specific brief (genre, BPM, constraint, banned words) -> a line only you would post

beats.narration_text: Lyrics get their own file, because craft rules are not caption rules. Syllable cadence against a reference track. Rhyme scheme. Chorus lines ending on open vowels. One emotion per section. The point isn't that a machine writes your songs — it's that when you use one to get unstuck, it gets stuck in YOUR forms, not its defaults.

illustration.spec: Onda code-block: lyric-writer skill excerpt — syllable cadence ±1 vs reference, rhyme scheme, open-vowel chorus endings, one emotion per section, running banned-images list

beats.narration_text: Now the paper, because this is your livelihood. Under current U.S. Copyright Office guidance, unmodified machine-generated lyrics and melodies may not be copyrightable — the line is human authorship. And the major distributors now ask you to disclose AI content. The safe pattern is also the honest one: it proposes, you rewrite, before anything touches a master. Check your distributor's current language before you release. That's not fear. That's owning your catalog.

illustration.spec: A master tape with a signature line; a rewrite arrow between 'AI draft' and 'released work'; distributor disclosure checkboxes

beats.narration_text: Step back and see what this episode actually built. Every act with a generator sounds like the generator now. The indie with a voice file sounds like themselves at scale. In a feed full of machine paste, a maintained voice is not a nicety. It's the moat.

illustration.spec: Many identical bot-caption cards vs one card in the artist's voice, standing apart

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. One page. Two files and a rule. Your taste, written once, obeyed at scale — and your name never signed by a machine.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, In Your Voice — one page
artifactLines: artist-brand-voice.md: tone · banned words · platform defaults · ask-before-naming · flag-don't-post numbers.
Specificity is the lever: the brief, not the model, decides whether it sounds like you.
lyric-writer.md: your cadence, your forms — for getting unstuck in your own shapes.
The paper: human authorship is the copyright line; distributors ask for AI disclosure — it proposes, you rewrite, you release.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this, answer honestly — the banned-words question is the one that matters — and keep the file it returns. That file is deck piece number three, and every recipe from episode two now speaks through it.

props.command: Interview me to build my artist-brand-voice skill file: my tone in three honest adjectives, ten words I'd never use, my platform defaults for Instagram, X, and industry-facing posts, and my two tripwires — ask before naming any person, flag rather than post any numeric claim. Then show me the file and three test captions written under it.
